title: Building FireStriker: Making Civic Tech Free
full_text:
My wife is the politically active one in our house. She's the person who shows up: rallies, election day volunteering, local organization meetings, city council sessions. She does the work. I'm a software engineer (she is too, but she's more politically active). I build things for other people during the day, and until recently, that was the extent of my contribution to the world.

Then we moved to El Paso, and everything changed.

The talk of the town in local political circles was a set of data centers being proposed in nearby communities. Big facilities, major implications for water usage, land, and the community's future. My wife dove in. She connected with organizers, attended meetings, tried to get the full picture.

But by the time people got organized enough to start showing up to city council meetings, the papers were already signed. The decision had been made. The window for public input had opened and closed while the community was still figuring out what was happening.

That moment stuck with me. Not because the outcome was unusual (it isn't), but because the reason was so fixable. People cared. They were willing to show up. They just didn't know soon enough.

The Notification Problem

What happened in the El Paso area wasn't a failure of civic will. It was a failure of civic infrastructure.

The organizations trying to mobilize around the data center issue were doing everything they could with the tools they had: group chats, shared Google Docs, someone manually checking the city council agenda every few days. The same improvised setup that grassroots groups everywhere rely on.

The problem is that this setup doesn't scale to the speed of government. A committee hearing gets scheduled. An agenda item gets added. A vote gets moved up. If nobody catches it in time, the window closes. And for community organizations without dedicated staff, "catching it in time" means someone happened to check the right website on the right day.

Meanwhile, the organizations on the other side of these decisions (developers, corporations, industry groups) have professional lobbyists with $30,000-per-year legislative tracking platforms. They know the moment a bill moves. They know exactly which officials to contact and when. They don't miss windows because their tools don't let them.

The gap isn't effort. It's tooling.

Why I Started Building

I've spent my career as a software engineer helping other people's visions come to life. Most of the time, honestly, I've been building tools that don't solve actual human problems. Dashboards for metrics nobody checks. Features designed to move product KPIs rather than help real people do real things.

After moving to El Paso, I started looking at what was available for civic tech. What could a grassroots organization actually use to stay on top of legislation, organize members, manage events, collect dues, and communicate, all without a real budget?

The answer: nothing. At least, nothing coherent.

On one end, there are enterprise platforms like Quorum ($30,000 to $100,000 per year), FiscalNote ($20,000 to $80,000 per year), and Capitol Canary ($15,000 to $50,000 per year). These tools are genuinely powerful. They track every bill, monitor floor speeches, flag engagement windows. They're also priced for organizations with dedicated government affairs departments.

On the other end, there are affordable tools like Action Network and Mobilize that handle events and email but have zero legislative intelligence. And then there's NationBuilder, which starts at $29 per month but scales quickly and still doesn't include any legislation tracking or government meeting data.

What most grassroots organizations actually use is the Frankenstein stack: Google Sheets for the member list, Mailchimp for email, Eventbrite for events, Venmo for dues, Facebook Groups for communication, and a government website checked manually for legislative updates. Seven disconnected tools that don't talk to each other.

I knew I could build something better. So I started.

Building Civic Tech Free

FireStriker is a free civic engagement and legislative intelligence platform. That sentence is doing a lot of work, so let me unpack it.

Civic engagement means the operational stuff: member management, events, dues and donations (a lot of platforms take a cut of this, we don't), communications, and organization profiles. The basics that every community group needs and currently cobbles together from five different free tools.

Legislative intelligence means the stuff that's been locked behind enterprise paywalls: bill tracking across federal and state legislatures, topic monitoring with stance detection that classifies whether a legislator supports or opposes your issue, engagement intelligence that tells you which officials to contact and when, and government meeting tracking synced from real government sources with plain-language summaries of what happened.

Free means free. Not a free trial. Not a free tier with the useful features paywalled. Not "free but we take 5% of your donations." Free. The processing fees for dues and donations go to Stripe. FireStriker doesn't touch them.

The pricing decision was the easiest one I made. The organizations that need these tools the most are the ones with the smallest budgets. A tenant association with 30 members. A union local running on minimal dues. A neighborhood group trying to track a zoning fight. Any price point, no matter how reasonable, creates a barrier for exactly the people I'm trying to help.

The Hard Part

I'm going to be honest about what building this looks like.

I work my day job during the day. I work on FireStriker at night and on the weekends. Early mornings, late nights, weekends spent writing code instead of doing basically anything else. My wife is supportive (she understands the problem better than anyone), but the time commitment is real.

And the hardest part isn't the code. It's the uncertainty. I don't know if anyone will actually use this. I don't know if it will have the impact I'm hoping for. I don't know if a solo developer building a free platform can actually change how smaller, less-funded organizations do politics.

What I do know is that the problem is real. I watched it play out in my own community. People who cared, who were willing to show up, who lost because they didn't have the right tools at the right time. That's not a hypothetical. That's the desert, baby.

So I keep building.

What FireStriker Does

For the civic tech folks and indie hackers reading this, here's the concrete feature set:

Member management with roles, status tracking, and privacy controls

Events with RSVPs, capacity limits, recurring schedules, check-in, and calendar export

Dues and donations through Stripe, zero platform fee, sliding scale support

Communication feed with posts, comments, reactions, and direct messaging

Legislation tracking across federal and state legislatures, searchable by keyword, topic, sponsor, or committee

Topic monitoring that watches what legislators are saying about your issues and classifies their stance

Engagement intelligence that identifies which officials to contact, when the engagement window is open, and which of your members are constituents of the target officials

Government meeting tracking synced from government sources with structured summaries of decisions, votes, and action items

All of it connected. The member who RSVPs to your event is the same member who gets a legislative alert because they live in a targeted district. That connection, the thing the Frankenstein stack can never deliver, is the entire point.

Why I'm Sharing This

I'm building in public because civic tech shouldn't be a black box. If you're going to trust a platform with your organization's members, donations, and legislative strategy, you should know who built it and why.

I'm one developer. I spent years building tools that didn't matter for problems that didn't exist. FireStriker is my attempt to use the skills I've picked up over a long career to solve problems for everyday people. For organizations like the ones my wife has been part of her entire adult life. For the groups that show up to city council meetings and deserve to know about the vote before it's already happened.

The platform is launching soon. If you're an organizer, an activist, a union steward, or just someone who thinks civic engagement tools shouldn't cost more than your organization's annual budget, I'd love for you to try it.

FireStriker is a free civic engagement and legislative intelligence platform for community organizations, unions, PACs, and activists.
